name: Built-in Node.js Test Runner
description: Use the built-in node --test runner for unit and integration testing without installing external test frameworks.
trendingReason: The built-in test runner (stable since Node 20) eliminates the need for Jest or Mocha in many projects, reducing dependencies.

steps.title: Create a test file
steps.description: Write a test file using the built-in node:test module with describe/it syntax.

steps.title: Run tests with the built-in runner
steps.description: Execute all test files matching the default pattern using node --test.
steps.tip: By default, node --test finds files matching **/*.test.{js,mjs,cjs} and **/*-test.{js,mjs,cjs} patterns.

steps.title: Run specific test files or use glob patterns
steps.description: Target specific test files or directories instead of running the full suite.

steps.title: Use mocking capabilities
steps.description: Use the built-in mock module to create spies, stubs, and mock functions.

steps.title: Generate test coverage reports
steps.description: Run tests with coverage enabled to see which lines of code are tested.
steps.tip: Coverage reports show per-file line, branch, and function coverage percentages directly in the terminal.

faq.question: Should I switch from Jest to the built-in test runner?
faq.answer: For simple projects, the built-in runner eliminates a dependency. For projects needing snapshot testing, complex mocking, or extensive plugin ecosystems, Jest still has advantages. Evaluate based on your needs.

faq.question: Does the built-in runner support watch mode?
faq.answer: Yes. Use node --test --watch to automatically re-run tests when files change. Available since Node.js 19.

faq.question: Can I use the built-in runner with TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. Use a loader: node --test --loader ts-node/esm 'tests/**/*.test.ts'. Or compile TypeScript first and run the JS output.
